Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) UMAKANT DANGAT ... Commissioner of Agriculture, Maharashtra State, Pune - 411 001 FOREWORD -pest surveillance and pest advisory project for some horticultural crops like banana, mango and pomegranate was launched during the year 2011-12 by Commissionerate of Agriculture Maharashtra State. The objectives of the project are pest surveillance, training of field staff about crop pests, crop diseases, data entry and issuing of pest management advisories based on economic threshold level for reducing pest densities by using eco friendly and environmentally compatible strategies. In the current era of globalization, the major concern before the nation is quality of harvested fruits and profitability to farmers as well as the concern of pesticide residues and pest infestation in harvested produce. Policy makers weighed the need for quality of produce and competiveness on international agriculture output market against the negative externalities of pesticides and presence of pest stages in the harvested produce that can damage the sustainability of the fruit production and export potential. Maharashtra enjoys prominent position on country’ horticulture map with more than 15 lakh hectare area under fruit crops. At the national level the state ranks 2nd in the fruit production after Tamil Nadu. Despite the production potential, the productivity of many fruit crops in Maharashtra is low as compared with national productivity and also average productivity of other states. Though the productivity of banana and pomegranate in Maharashtra is highest in the country, it has witnessed declining trend in productivity since 2010-11 as compared to 2008-09. Many reasons have been listed for declining trend in productivity of fruit crops. However, pests and diseases had their own share for lower productivity. Prominent among them are sigatoka disease in banana, hoppers and fruit fly in mango and bacterial blight in pomegranate. Maharashtra is having Agri Export Zones for many horticultural crops like mango, pomegranage, banana etc. The thrust for raising productivity and preventing losses due to pests resulted into high use of pesticide which confounds the aim of integrated pest management and action threshold based application of pesticides. Department of Agriculture and Marketing, Government of Maharashtra recognized the need to invest in and pioneered implementation of -pest surveillance programme and to promote farmers’ participatory pest management practices. It’ wider uptake of judicious, timely application of right kind of pesticides in fruit crops to raise the potential for export as well as alleviate the consumer awareness about health hazards arising from high level of pesticide iii

NCIPM residues in the harvested produce. It has also recognized an opportunity to forge stronger and more substantive partnership with the National Centre for Integrated Pest Management (NCIPM-ICAR), National Research Centre for Pomegranate, Solapur, National Research Centre for Banana, Trichy, Mahatma Phule Agriculture University, Rahuri and Dr. Balasaheb Sawant Agriculture University, Dapoli for surveillance plan and expertise in pest management. The objective of -pest surveillance is scientific control of pests and diseases through meticulous field observations by uploading in time for experts’ scientific advice and timely implementation of management interventions based on these advisories so as to ensure ecofriendness, economic, timely pest control measures with minimum pesticide residue levels. This goal of timely application of pesticides was achieved through three-pronged approaches viz., () Recording of pest/ pest incidence data at weekly intervals in structured sheet through pest scouts at field level in villages, (ii) Collation of such pest data into one central server for analysis and (iii) Interpretation of such data to provide guidance and issue pest advisory on the basis of economic threshold levels. This programme acted as vehicle to catalyse and facilitate the collaboration among policy makers (Department of Agriculture and Marketing, Government of Maharashtra) on one hand and research institutes (NCIPM, NRC Banana and NRC Pomegranate) and state agriculture universities (MPKV and Dr. BSKKV) on the other side in the planning and implementation of -pest surveillance project. The success of implementation of -pest surveillance project lies in proper identification of pests and diseases, timely collateral entry of such data into the centralized server, its accessibility by the experts, proper maintenance of software and suitable delivery of pest management advisory to the farmers and end users. The meticulous pest data entry, prompt monitoring and approval of such data by field staff of State Department, available expertise of national research institutes and state agricultural universities in the field of pest management and trainings enabled the herculean task of implementation of -pest surveillance easier, commendable and exemplary. appreciate the team work and efforts taken by national research institutes and state agricultural universities as well as implementing officers and staff of Department of Agriculture and Marketing, Government of Maharashtra in the implementation of -pest surveillance and wish that dedicated efforts made by all has achieved the goals set for the project and shall continue to deliver and provide effective farmer-friendly results in the near future. (Umakant Dangat) iv

Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) Introduction India is the second largest producer of fruits in the world, just behind China. The area under fruit cultivation in 2011-12 was .704 million hectares with total production of 76. million tonnes (Source: Ministry of Agriculture, GOI, and website: www.nhb.gov.in).The major fruits grown in India are mango, banana, citrus, guava, grape, pineapple, pomegranate, and apple. Although fruits are grown throughout the country, the major fruit growing states are Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh and Gujarat. India ranks first among banana producing countries in the world with production of 29. million tonnes from an area of 830 thousand ha. In the year 2010-11, Tamil Nadu lead in area (125. thousand ha), production (8253.40 thousand tonnes) and productivity (65. mt/ ha). The other major banana growing states are Maharashtra, Karnataka, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Bihar, Uttar Pradsh, Madhya Pradesh and Assam (www.apeda.gov.in). In Maharashtra, productivity (tonnes/ha) has declined in year 2010-11(52.) as compared to year 2009-10 (61.17) and 2008-09 (62.) (NHB 2011-12). Major reason assigned for declined productivity was the severe loss caused by one disease .. the sigatoka leaf spot that caused severe decline in productivity. Mango, referred to as the ‘King of fruits’ is the most important commercially grown fruit in India and ranks first among mango producing countries accounting for 50 percent of the world’ production. India has the richest collection of mango cultivars. Major mango producing states are Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, West Bengal, Maharashtra, Gujarat, Karnataka, Kerala and Tamil Nadu. Uttar Pradesh ranks first in production with ,841.00 thousand tonnes. Area under mango cultivation in Maharashtra has not changed over the years, however both the production (000MT) and productivity (HA/MT) has declined considerably in year 2010-11(331 and .) as compared to year 2009-2010 (597 and .) and year 2008-09 (712. and .). The climatic change in Konkan belt, coupled with erratic winters and damage due to pests and diseases have led to this fall. Pomegranate was considered as minor fruit in India till the year 1986, but gained importance due to its economic and nutraceutical values. steady increase in pomegranate area and production of India was witnessed till 2007-08 when it occupied an area of 123. thousand ha and production of 884. thousand tonnes. In 2010-11 the area decreased to 107. thousand ha and production to 743. thousand tonnes. Picture of avarage productivity of Maharashtra was grimmer as compared to all India figures. Average productivity of pomegranate in state is . tonnes/ha which is very low compared with other states like Tamil Nadu (27. tonnes/ha), Karnataka (10.), Gujarat (10. tonnes/ha), Andhra Pradesh (10.) and national average of . tonnes/ha (Indian Horticultural Data Base 2012). Moreover the productivity of pomegranate in Maharashtra stated for the last years remained static. This has been attributed to diseases and insect pests that were of little economic importance in the past, but have now emerged as new threats to the growers in some localities. Apart from bacterial blight, wilt, thrips and fruit borer along with new pests like some fungal fruit rots and spots, and fruit sucking moth required immediate attention.

NCIPM Awareness among growers to identify and manage diseases and insect pests of fruit crops is essential for higher productivity as well as for quality produce. This requires long term strategies to manage risks such as pests and diseases following the principles of integrated pest management. Farmers tend to overuse pesticides which are detrimental to human health as well as to the environment. They have to be advised to apply need based pesticides. Also, the fruit growing areas are widespread in Maharashtra and it is impossible to convey proper warning at the time of pest infestation. By the time management measures are underway, large areas gets devastated. Hence, comprehensive method has to be devised so that such catastrophe can be avoided. In view of the futuristic requirements as well as strategies to meet unforeseen circumstances and to increase the yield by reducing pest infestation, an “-Pest Surveillance” programme has been initiated by Maharashtra State Govt along with National Centre for Integrated Pest Management (NCIPM), New Delhi. Other collaborators are, NRC Banana, NRC Pomegranate, MPKV and Dr. BSKKV. It provides field-specific information on pest pressure and crop injury leading to take appropriate decision about selection and application of pest management procedures by constant monitoring and scouting useing -Pest Surveillance. It is basically web based Decision Support System (DSS) for timely collection of data with regard to pest population and preparation of reports which greatly facilitates the execution of pest management activity. Besides it helps in creation of data base on pests that may also be used to ascertain the impact of climate change on the incidence of pest in the mandated crops. Present compilation envisage the procedure to be followed for implementation of -Pest Surveillance, transmission of pest advisory to farmers, nature and symptoms of damage due to the pests for these three important fruit crops grown in Maharashtra and pest management strategies to reduce the pest densities in environmentally compatible way. Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) BANANA Major insect pests Thrips (Chaetanaphothrips signipennis) Symptoms (cid:) Distinctive reddish brown oval stains on the finger, which can extend the entire length. (cid:) In severe cases peel splits and the exposed flesh quickly discolors. Pest identification (cid:) Egg: Kidney shaped, invisible to naked eye, laid just below the fruit or pseudostem surface. (cid:) Larva: They are wingless creamy white, smaller but have the same shape as the adult. (cid:) Pupa:Pupae are white like the larvae, can crawl, mm in length. Rust thrips infested fingers (cid:) Adult:They are slender, creamy yellow to golden brown with delicate feathery wings and . mm long. Their wings have dark, eye-like spots at the base and are fringed; when the wings are folded, the adult appears to have black line down its back. Biology of the pest Adult banana rust thrips reproduce sexually. After mating, females lay eggs in plant tissues where the thrips feed and hatch in – days. The newly hatched yellow nymphs feed for few days before molting and after –10 days, mature nymphs migrate off the host plant into the soil and undergo pupation with the adult emerging in –10 days. The entire life cycle is completed in approximately 28 days. Management practices (cid:) At the time of shooting, cover the bunch firmly (without any space between polythene cover and bunch) with 100 gauge thickness polythene sleeve having to 10% holes. (cid:) Remove the male flower buds after opening of all hands. (cid:) When the bud is in upright position, bud injection with imidachloprid 17.8EC @ 2ml

NCIPM chemical solution (. ml of chemical dissolved in 500 ml of water) per bud using disposable syringe (cid:) Spray the bunch with chlorpyriphos @ . ml/ litre of water/ acetamiprid 20SP (.0025 %) .. .25 / 10 liter of water along with sticking agent (@ .5ml/litre of water, two times ie one at the time of opening of hands and second spray after all the hands opened. (cid:) Verticillium lecanii ( 108 cfu/) 3g/ lit + 1ml Milk + 1ml sticker or NSKE % + sticker can be taken up as second spray. Aphids (Pentalonia nigronervosa) Symptoms (cid:) Banana leaves are bunched into rosette appearance with leaf margins becoming wavy and upward rolling thereby reducing the growth and vigour of plant. Severly infected plants do not produce bunches and act as vector of bunchy top disease. (cid:) Noticed in colonies on leaf axils and pseudostem Pest Identification (cid:) Eggs: Egg stage not present and young ones are born live (cid:) Nymph: Oval or slightly elongated, reddish brown with six segmented antennae Aphid infestation (cid:) Adult: Reddish to dark brown/ almost black, shiny, small to medium sized. Biology of the pest It is phloem feeder that uses its long stylet to pierce plant tissues to suck the sap directly from the vessels. Reproduction is entirely parthenogenetic (without mating). Females give birth directly to live young female. The life cycle from nymph to adult is completed in to 16 days. Management practices (cid:) Adopt clean cultivation using healthy and pest free suckers (cid:) Remove the affected plants and do not take up ratoon and inter crops (cid:) Drench the petioles, furled leaves, whorls or young suckers with soapy water or insecticidal soap (cid:) Spray dimethoate (75ml/100lit) or acephate (.3g/lit) on infested plants and suckers (cid:) Use of braconid wasps, Lysiphlebius testaceipes as parasitoid

Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) (cid:) Releasing of predators such as lady bird beetles and lace wings (cid:) Application of bio control agent Beauveria bassiana in the banana fields Corm Weevil (Cosmopolites sordidus) Symptoms (cid:) The young grub tunnels into the base of suckers, roots and rhizome/corm. (cid:) Presence of larval tunnels on the entire length of pseudostems. (cid:) Yellowing and withering of leaves, reduced plant vigour, root destruction, reduced fruit production and are easily blown over by the wind. Corm weevil ) Weevil, ) Grub Pest Identification (cid:) Egg: White colour, elongate to oval, about to mm long and present on upper part of rhizome (cid:) Grub: Legless grubs, creamy white with red head having strong mouthparts, stout and distinctly curved. (cid:) Pupa: They are white and found in corm tunnels. (cid:) Adult: . Weevils newly emerged are red brown which turns almost black after few days, hard-shelled having long curved snout and about 10 to 16 mm long. . They are active at night, may live up to two years but are very sensitive to dehydration and will die within 48 hours if kept in dry substrate. Biology of the pest Females lay eggs singly in small cavities at the base of pseudostem, in the upper part of the corm, in roots near the soil surface and at the end of cut stems (stumps). They hatch after to days and pupation takes place in holes bored by the grubs and adults emerge from the pupae to days after pupation. Management practices (cid:) Practice clean cultivation with the suckers pruned periodically and infested clumps are removed and destroyed (cid:) Crop rotation with non host crops like paddy and sugarcane (cid:) Ensure proper fertilization and weed removal

NCIPM (cid:) Use of pheromone trap @ 16 traps /ha (cid:) Disc-on-stump traps can be used for trapping weevils. (cid:) Application of bio control agents, Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae, causes more than 90% mortality of the weevils (cid:) Application of carbofuran 3G @ 40g or rugby 10G @ 10g/plant neem cake @ 500 /plant at planting and then at three months after planting. (cid:) Sucker dipping in triazophos solution (. ml in lit water) for about 20 minutes kills the eggs and grubs (cid:) Remove the entire plants after harvest and treat the pit with carbaryl (1g/lit) or chlorpyrifhos (.5ml/lit) Stem Weevil / pseudostem borer (Odoiporus longicollis) Symptoms (cid:) Presence of small pinhead-sized holes on the stem. (cid:) Jelly exudation on the stem is the initial symptom of damage. (cid:) Due to secondary infection of pathogens, rotting occurs and foul odour is emitted. (cid:) After flowering, when tunnelling occurs in the true stem and peduncle, the fruits do not develop properly. Pest identification (cid:) Egg: Creamy, cylindrical with rounded ends. (cid:) Grub: They are fleshy, yellowish white dark brown head and apodous. Stem weevil (cid:) Pupa: Pupate in fibrous cocoon, pale yellow colour and is exarate. (cid:) Adult: Brownish black and measure 23-39 mm. Biology of the pest Gravid females lay eggs at random on cut ends of pseudostem.The incubation period ranges from to days. The larvae pass through five instars with the fifth instar larvae entering non-feeding pre-pupal stage and after 15 days of pupation, adult emerges. The total life cycle completes about in 55-60 days. Management practices (cid:) After harvesting the bunch, remove the Pseudostem from ground level and destroy them in order to avoid it serving as breeding site for the pest.

Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) (cid:) Uproot and burn infested plants. (cid:) Use Disc-on-stump or longitudinal Pseudostem traps @ 100/ha for trapping weevils. (cid:) Swabbing with chlorpyripos .5ml/ + adjuvant 1ml/ on the stem prevents infestation of banana stem weevil. (cid:) When jelly exudation is noticed, inject 2ml triazophos solution (350 ml in 150 ml water). Two injections per plant at and feet above the ground level till flowering. The injection needle should enter only two or three leaf sheaths and should not touch the central core. Burrowing Nematode (Radopholus similis) Symptoms (cid:) Reddish-brown to black, elongated discoloured area seen parallel to the root axis which eventually blacken and die. (cid:) Lack of vigour in infested plants and poor fruiting observed. (cid:) Infested plants are readily toppled and the roots get exposed. Burrowing nematode [courtesyinternet] Biology of the pest The nematode is migratory endoparasite, which completes its life cycle in about 21 days at 25ºC in the root corm tissues. Females and juvenile stages both attack and enter host roots, especially near the tip of the roots. Males do not feed, as their stylets are weak. Females lay an average of - eggs each day. Management practices (cid:) Crop rotation with non host crops (cid:) Adopt soil solarization. (cid:) Remove all the black or discolored spots on the corm and root tissue, leaving only clean white tissues (cid:) Grow marigold as repellent and trap crop in the inter space (cid:) Use of neem cake @ 500g/ plant along with anyone of the bioagents Trichoderma viride/ Paecilomyces lilacinus/ Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 20 / plant. (cid:) Application of carbofuran @ 50 / plant. (cid:) Nematicide rugby 10G applied at 10g/ plant during 3rd and 5th month reduced the nematode population and increased the yield.

NCIPM Major diseases Sigatoka Leaf Spot (Mycosphaerella eumusae) Symptoms (cid:) Small, pale spots on leaves that turn dark purplish black with grey centres (cid:) Disease more prevalent on shallow, poorly drained soil (cid:) Severity of symptoms depicts burnt appearance of leaves (cid:) Poor filling and quality of bunches (cid:) Fruits don’ mature uniformly Leaf Spot Pathogen dissemination (cid:) Wind, rain water and old dried infected leaves carrying conidia help to spread the disease Management practices (cid:) The field must be kept weed free and clean. Follow either hand weeding/ harrowing till months after planting or use herbicide- glyphosate -10 ml/ litre of water + 25 of urea or ammonium sulphate/ tank or by intercropping with cowpea. (cid:) While planting, optimum/ recommended spacing (. . ) must be followed (cid:) Provide adequate drainage facility whenever it is required (cid:) Apply only the recommended dose of fertilizer - , , / plant (200:40:400) as per the schedule + 25g azospirilium + 25 phosphorussolublizing bacteria. Application of Neem cake @. to kg / plant may also be applied. Potash can be applied 10 to 20% more. Micronutrient mixture 10 / plant in 3rd month and 5th month after planting must be applied. (cid:) Remove of disease infected leaves or part of leaves & destroy it outside the orchard (cid:) No dried leaves should be hanging around the plant (cid:) The following pesticides may be applied as soon as the symptom appears on the leaves. The interval between two sprays may be 20 to 25 days ) Mineral oil 10 ml + propiconazole .% (1ml per litre of water) ) Mineral oil 10 ml + carbendazim .% (1g per litre of water) ) Mineral oil 10 ml + tridemorph .% (1g per litre of water) ) Mineral oil 10 ml + companion .% (1g per litre of water)

Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) ) Mineral oil 10 ml + propiconazole .% (1ml per litre of water) ) Mineral oil 10 ml + carbendazim .% (1g per litre of water) ) Mineral oil 10 ml + tridemorph .% (1ml per litre of water) ) Mineral oil 10 ml + propiconazole .% (1ml per litre of water) Note: . When oil (Banole – banana spray oil) is used no sticking agent is required . Spray must be done in such way that the spray should cover ) both the side of the leaves, and ) all the leaves including the top most unfurled leaf. . Only the recommended dose of chemicals should be applied to avoid development of resistance to fungicides, to reduce the cost of cultivation, to reduce the environmental pollution and also to avoid residue problems in the produce. Anthracnose (Colletotrichum musae) Symptoms (cid:) Small, circular, black spots develop on the affected fruits. (cid:) At the initial stage dark brown patches on immature fruits (cid:) Severe infestation leads to shriveled and black coloured rotten fruits covered with pink spore masses, which gradually spreads and affects the whole bunch. Pathogen dissemination (cid:) Air-borne conidia and numerous insects visiting banana flowers spread the disease. Anthracnose (cid:) High atmospheric temperature, humidity, wounds and bruises caused in the fruit and susceptibility of the variety favours disease development. Management practices (cid:) Adopt clean cultivation and maintain proper field sanitation. (cid:) Harvest bunches at correct stage of maturity. (cid:) When all the hands are opened, the distal bud should be removed to prevent infection. (cid:) Transported bunches should be stored carefully at 14ºc without causing any bruises. (cid:) Avoid contamination in collecting places, during transport and in ripening rooms. (cid:) Preharvest spray with carbendazim .% two times at monthly interval. (cid:) Postharvest dipping of fruits carbendazim 400 ppm or benomyl 1000 ppm.

NCIPM Banana Bract Mosaic Virus (BBMV) Symptoms (cid:) Presence of spindle shaped pinkish to reddish streaks on pseudostem, petiole, midrib and peduncle (cid:) At emergence, suckers exhibit unusual reddish brown streaks and separation of leaf sheath from central axis (cid:) Crowding of leaves at crown which appears like palm leaf frill with elongated peduncle and half filled hands. Pathogen dissemination (cid:) Aphid vectors such as Aphis gossypii, Pentalonia nigronervosa and Rhopalosiphum maidis transmit the disease. (cid:) Disease spread is also through suckers. Management practices (cid:) Use disease free planting material. BBMV (cid:) Rouging of diseased plants. (cid:) Practice clean cultivation and remove weeds which might harbour virus (cid:) Control of insect vector by spraying dimethoate (75 ml / 100 ) or acephate (. /). (cid:) Apply more amount of organic manures especially cakes and 25% more of recommended fertilizers Banana Bunchy Top Virus (BBTV) Symptoms (cid:) Yellowing of leaf margin initially and dark green streaks on the leaves. (cid:) New leaves emerge with difficulty, are narrower than normal, are wavy rather than flat, and have yellow (chlorotic) leaf margins. (cid:) Leaves form bunch at the top. (cid:) Usually fruiting doesn’ occur in severely infected banana plants but if produced, the banana hands and fingers are likely to be distorted and twisted. BBTV 10

Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) Pathogen dissemination (cid:) The virus concentration is more in phloem sap. (cid:) It is transmitted by infected suckers and banana aphid. Management practices (cid:) Use of virus free planting materials. (cid:) Rouging and removal of infected banana plants. (cid:) Practice clean cultivation. (cid:) Avoid banana cultivation in sugarcane and cucurbitaceous areas as sugarcane mosaic virus or cucurbit mosaic virus can easily spread to banana. (cid:) The diseased trees should be injected with ml of fernoxone solution (50g in 400 ml of water) or insertion of fernoxone capsules (containing 200 to 400 mg of chemical per capsule) into the pseudostem to kill the virus infected plants. Cigar End Rot (Verticillium theobromae) Symptoms (cid:) Black necrosis from the perianth to the tip of immature fruits causing shrinkage and folding of the tissues. (cid:) Fingers that appear rotten are full of grey conidia, which look like the ash of cigar. Pathogen dissemination (cid:) Warm and moist conditions favour the disease occurrence and the disease spread is high in old and badly maintained plantations. Cigar end rot Management practices (cid:) Avoid overcrowding of plants ensuring enough aeration in plantations. (cid:) Young bunches should be exposed to light and air and the stray bracts should be removed especially during wet weather. (cid:) Improved sanitation helps in the reduction of the disease. (cid:) Removal of pistil and perianth by hand immediately after the fruits are formed. (cid:) Pistils should be removed to 11 days after bunch emergence. (cid:) Spraying the peduncle with carbendazim at .% after shoot emergence. 11

NCIPM Erwinia Rot (Erwinia carotovora) Symptoms (cid:) Affected young suckers show rotting and emits foul odour. (cid:) The rot progresses up the Pseudostem causing internal decay often with vascular discoloration. (cid:) If affected plants are pulled, it comes out from the collar region leaving the corm with their roots in the soil. (cid:) Infection at late stage shows splitting of Pseudostem in Erwinia rot infected corm some cultivars. (cid:) Yellowish to reddish ooze seen when affected plants are cut open at collar region. (cid:) Soft rotting may spread radially towards growing point through the cortical tissues. Pathogen dissemination (cid:) The pathogen being soil borne enters through wounds and also through leaf sheath of suckers. (cid:) Hot and damp weather with plenty of rainfall trigger the disease development. (cid:) Water is required for the bacteria to invade into the plant. Management practices (cid:) Use disease free suckers for planting. (cid:) Removal and destruction of infected plants. (cid:) Rainy season planting should be avoided. (cid:) Grow sunnhemp or cowpea as intercrop to provide shade to the banana plants. (cid:) Give regular irrigation only in either morning or evening hrs. (cid:) Apply the plants with bleaching powder at 8g/ mat at 10 to 15 days interval for - times. Panama Wilt (Fusarium oxysporum . sp. cubense) Symptoms (cid:) Visual observations show yellowing of lower leaves, longitudinal splitting of Pseudostem and wilting of plants. (cid:) Advanced stages of disease shows plants with spiky appearance due to prominent upright apical leaves. 12

Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) (cid:) The xylem (water conducting) vessels turn reddish- brown as the fungus grows through the tissues. Pathogen dissemination (cid:) It is soil borne disease and the fungus enters the roots through the fine laterals. (cid:) Infected rhizomes or suckers, farm implements, irrigation water is responsible for the spread of the fungus. Management practices (cid:) Grow wilt resistant cultivars in endemic areas and avoid Fusarium wilt infected plant susceptible varieties. NCIPM POMEGRANATE Major insect pests Pomegranate fruit borer (Deudorix isocrates) Pomegranate fruit borer is distributed all over India and Asia. It is the most widespread, polyphagous and destructive pest of pomegranate. The adult female lays egg on buds, flower and young fruits. On hatching, the caterpillar bores into the fruit and feeds on the pulp. The fruits then rot and drop off on the soil. The adult males are glossy bluish and brownish violet and in case of females conspicuous orange patch on the forewings is seen. The damage of fruit borer is seen through out the year irrespective of the bahar. Symptoms (cid:) The female butterfly lays eggs on flowers, buds and the calyx of developing fruits, after hatching, caterpillars enter the fruit and feed on the pulp. (cid:) The striking symptom is the odious smell and excreta of caterpillars coming out of the entry holes ultimately leading to fruit rot. Fruit borer Adult Larva Pupa Bored hole on fruit with excreta Pest identification (cid:) Egg: They are laid singly on tender leaves, stalks and flower buds. (cid:) Larva: Full-grown larvae are dark brown with short hair and white patches all over the body and measures about 16 to 20mm long (cid:) Pupa: Pupation occurs either inside the damaged fruits or on the stalk holding it (cid:) Adult: Adults are glossy bluish in the case of male and brownish violet in the case of female with conspicuous orange patch on the forewings. 36

Major Diseases Bacterial blight (Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. punicae) Bacterial blight has been of wide occurrence in India, resulting in economic losses in all major pomegranate growing areas of Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Himachal Pradesh and Rajasthan. Outside India it is reported from Pakistan and South Africa. Disease build up is rapid during rainy season. High disease severity is observed from July to October. Temperatures between 25 to 35oC coupled with humidity above 50%, rains and wind favour rapid disease development. The disease affects all plant parts, but is most destructive on fruits. Symptoms (cid:) On leaf the disease appears in the form of regular to irregular dark brown to brown- black necrotic spots, surrounded by yellow hallo or water soaked margin. The infected leaves turn yellow and drop on the ground. 40

Pests of Fruits (Banana, Mango and Pomegranate) (cid:) On twigs and branches, brown to black water soaked lesions start around the nodes. These lesions develop into large cankers and may break on slight pressure. (cid:) On fruits disease starts with water soaked lesions on the skin surface, which turn dark brown to black. Spots enlarge and merge with each other to cover large area; however, infection is restricted to the rind. Small cracks appear on the spots and in severe cases entire fruit splits open along the lesions. The spots may be covered with thin shining white encrustation consisting of Bacterial blight on () Leaves () Twig () Fruit bacteria. Survival and Dissemination (cid:) This bacterium can survive on infected plant debris lying in and around the orchards for more than months. (cid:) It can also survive for several years on stem cankers on plants in orchard or in dormant buds; these are major sources of primary inoculum for next crop season. (cid:) It cannot survive in soil for more than 25-30 days without its host. (cid:) Planting material prepared from blight affected orchard or air borne inoculum from neighbouring infected orchard during rainy season is primary source of inoculum in new area (cid:) The secondary spread of bacteria is mainly through rain and spray splashes, irrigation water, pruning tools, man and insect vectors. Management practices (cid:) Plant new orchard with disease free planting material (cid:) Adopt orchard sanitation, proper plant nutrition and irrigation, cultural practices and chemical spray schedules to check diseases and insect pests. (cid:) Spray Bordeaux mixture (.% except % just after pruning and rest period), altered with spray of streptocycline (5g/10 ) or -bromo, -nitro propane-, -diol (5g/10 ) mixed with copper based formulations like copper oxychloride or copper hydroxide (20-25g/ 10 ) and spreader sticker (.5ml/) at 10-15 days interval depending on weather conditions. Depending on fungal problems present in the orchard copper based formulations may be replaced with appropriate fungicides. 41

NCIPM Pomegranate wilt: (Ceratocystis fimbriata, Xyleborus fornicates and . perforans Fusarium oxysporum, Rhizoctonia solani and Meloidogyne incognita) Wilt is the second most important disease of pomegranate, adversely affecting pomegranate cultivation in India. The disease has been reported from Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh and Himachal Pradesh. Pomegranate vascular wilt is mainly caused due to fungus Ceratocystis fimbriata occasionally by other agents like Fusarium solani and . oxysporum. Root rot organisms viz. Macrophomina phaseolina, Phytophthora nicotianae, Rhizoctonia bataticola; root-knot nematode Meloidogyne incognita and shot hole borer -Xyleborus fornicates and . perforans - are the other agents which, alone or in association with . fimbriata result in drying and wilting of plants. Symptoms (cid:) Initially yellowing of leaves is observed in some twigs or branches, followed by drooping and drying of leaves, finally the entire tree dies. (cid:) Longitudinally or cross-section of root or lower part of affected stem shows dark bluish-black/grey/brown discolouration of the wood is seen if the pathogen is Ceratocystis, in Fusarium browning of only xylem is observed. (cid:) Pin holes are observed in the bark and wood when shoot hole borers are associated with wilt. (cid:) Macrophomina sp. destroy the feeder roots and result in root rots or Rhizoctonia sp. cause girdling of stems in nursery or young plants-resulting in wilting (cid:) In nematode infestations infected plants form knots on the roots. Initial yellowing in Advance stage Discoloration Pin holes due to Knots due to root knot nematode wilted plants of wilt of wood due to shot hole borer . fimbriata Survival and dissemination (cid:) Thick walled Aleurioconidia of . fimbriata, in soil, host and plant debris are the most common survival structures and primary source of inoculum. The fungus can survive in infected wood for several years. (cid:) Infected Planting material or the soil used for raising the nursery planting material generally are the primary source of infection for wilt organisms 42

NCIPM Implementation of electronic -pest (-pest) surveillance Surveillance Plan Cultivation of mango, pomegranate and banana is adversely affected due to damage by one or more of the above documented pests but few pests such as Sigatoka leaf spot and thrips in banana, hoppers, thrips, powdery mildew and anthracnose in mango and bacterial blight, wilt, fruit borer and , thrips in pomegranate were considered most important leading to economic losses and force the farmers to make repetitive sprays. To change from the situation of repetitive sprays to Economic Threshold Level (ETL) based application of pesticides, constant watch is required on pest activity. It was made possible with the help of information technology that helped to develop an - pest vigilance/ surveillance programme by recording pest activity data with the help of scouts and pest monitor employed by Department of Horticulture, Govt. of Maharashtra. The pest data recorded is fed in to the system and subsequently transferred to centralized server located at NCIPM, New Delhi. For assessing the potential damage caused by pest, criteria for hoppers is based on number/panicle/ shoots whereas for thrips before fruit formation is also on the basis of number as for hoppers but after fruit formation, it is recorded on - scale. Total number of fruits from pea nut stage onwards are recorded per shoot/ panicle selected. Mango Hoppers : Weekly observation on number of both nymphs and adults are recorded on selected shoots or panicles. Mean hoppers per shoot/panicle is calculated (Total No. of values / 20). Mango Thrips : Total number of fruits from pea nut stage onwards are recorded per shoot/ panicle selected. Observations on thrips population are recorded by tapping the panicles on white paper. As the fruiting starts, damage due to thrips on fruits is assesed. On the basis of surface area of fruits damaged by thrips, the observed fruits is placed in - scale as details below. Mean thrips per shoot/panicle per plant (Total No. of values / 20). Other pests: Record the name of other pests only Pomegranate Selection of orchards and trees Two fixed orchards and two random orchards /village are selected by one scout who cover two villages per day. In each orchard, 50 trees are observed by selecting trees at ten sites. Select orchard having at least one acre area and assigned fixed / and random /. Prefer villages at 10 Km distance, however, adjoining village is also considered if it has at least 50 ha area. Write the name of village as mentioned in office records. Name and contact number of Grower to be noted for fixed plots only. Methods of observations: Observations on bacterial blight, wilt, thrips and fruit borer are recorded in structured sheet (Annexure iii). Pick zigzag route across the orchard so as to represent the entire orchard area, and inspect plants each at 10 sites in an orchard for all diseases except wilt and 1plant at each site for insect pests, leaving border rows and plants. 51

Stage of crop on the day of survey: Pomegranate production is taken through out the year in Maharashtra, hence the crop can be at rest/stress/defoliation/flowering/fruiting/ready for harvest at the time of survey, disease/insect pest situation also varies with crop stage, hence should check the crop stage and note. Orchard Sanitation: Check Poor if full of weeds and fallen plant debris, Good if the basin and rows are almost free from weeds and plant debris, however some weeds may be seen along the bunds and plant debris dumped near the orchard, Excellent if the orchard has no weeds and fallen debris in and around the orchard Crop condition: Note the plant growth and foliage depending on age of crop. 53

NCIPM Foliage: Check Good if sufficient healthy green foliage with normal expanded leaves is observed with proper plant canopy, Average if in general foliage is green but some foliage is yellow/distorted or bunched leaves/affected with diseases and pests and Poor if heavy incidence of diseases and insects observed or foliage is not green and showing poor nutrition status. Fruit bearing: Observe fruit bearing depending on age Age Optimum No. of and canopy of plant. The optimum bearing for good (yrs) fruits/tree canopy is given in the table. For trees bearing fruits more 15-20 than this check Heavy, for trees with optimum bearing check 30-35 Optimum and for lower than optimum Check Poor 60-80 Fruit stage: If fruits have developed colour/on tapping > 100-120 the fruit you get metallic sound/arils are red and sweet to taste check ready for harvest otherwise check not ready for harvest Fruit Size: Write the size depending on size of 70% fruits, if >70% fruits are above 400g check King size, if 350-400g check Large, if 250-300g check Medium and below 250g writ check Small Fruit colour: Note fruit colour only if fruits are almost ready for harvest. The varieties Bhagawa/Arakta/Mridula/Ruby have red fruits and Ganesh has yellowish pink colour fruits. If the fruits have the normal varietal colour uniformly without any spots/scars check Excellent, if fruit colour deviates slightly from normal or has some scars/spots check Medium, if fruits have not developed normal varietal colour or have many spots or scars check Poor. Other Activities: Note down various activities of pruning, training, irrigation schedule, fertilizers and most common insecticides, fungicides, bactericides, bioformulations, botanicals etc used by the farmer in the schedule. Any other information relevant to the orchard performance (Good/bad) may be noted. GUIDE LINE TO PEST MONITOR Guide lines for selecting village/orchards/tree/plants and methods of observation are same as laid out for pest scout except pest monitor shall also make roving survey for all the three crops, select five orchards per day randomly and record observations in the structured sheet for Banana (Annexure iv), Mango (Annexure ) and pomegranate (Annexure vi). Digitization of such data on centralized server enable the pest managers to view the pest activity without loss of time and pass on the pest advisory. In order to facilitate the pest monitor about the rating and extent of pest damage caused due to various pests in each crop, guidelines are issued which are given in Table -. He also visits the orchards, wherever he comes to know about the sudden outbreak of pest activity and immediately report the incidence to pest approval officer. He is also responsible for supervising the work of pest scout to establish the truthfulness and accuracy of data. 54
